Brandon, here’s a concise overview of your repo: a terminal chat app (chat_terminal) that lets you interact with OpenAI or Anthropic models from the command line, sandbox and automate tasks in a user-designated directory, and persist conversation memory and notes across sessions. The core is chat.py, which handles model communication, sandboxed command execution, memory (chat.json, chat.md), and an auto-mode loop that lets the AI work through tasks until completion. It includes a tool/command API for the model to run local commands, manage files, and update notes, plus features like repo summarization (summarize_repo) and memory maintenance. Supporting files provide docs, tests, and auxiliary utilities: LICENSE (public domain), README.md (usage), test.py and scratch.py (helpers), .gitignore, VS Code launch.json, a Windows batch updater (jsk_update.bat) for another app, and sample docs in auto_mode_test (companion_plants.md, life_advice.txt) for testing or demonstration. The repo is designed for experimentation with iterative AI workflows in a local sandbox, with persistent context and optional auto-run capabilities.

- You asked whether evolution has noticeable effects within our lifetimes.
- I noted that evolution is ongoing and highlighted rapid genetic changes in response to environment, plus cases where plasticity plays a role.
- Examples discussed:
  - Peppered moths (industrial melanism) in Britain.
  - Darwin’s finches on the Galápagos (beak changes during droughts).
  - Three-spined sticklebacks (marine to freshwater forms, armor plates) over decades.
  - Guppies in Trinidad (life-history and coloration changes under predation).
  - Rock pocket mice (camouflage adaptations after lava flows).
  - Insects evolving pesticide resistance (e.g., Colorado potato beetle).
  - Climate-related shifts in phenology and, in some cases, genetic components.
- I explained why fast reproduction helps: more generations, more variation, stronger selection, though evolution also happens in longer-lived species and plasticity can mimic or accompany genetic change.
